title: Sex determination and sex-linked traits
examTip: Sex-linked inheritance questions almost always involve colour blindness or haemophilia. Be ready to draw a Punnett square using X and Y notation, identify carriers, and explain why males are more commonly affected. This is a reliable extended-answer topic.
introTips: The reason males get colour blindness much more often than females isn't luck — it's a direct consequence of having only one X chromosome. Once you see why, you'll never forget it.

Use the notation carefully: X with a superscript for the allele (X^B for dominant, X^b for recessive), and Y for the male sex chromosome. Getting your notation right means the Punnett square does the work for you.
Carrier females are a key concept here — they don't show the condition but can pass it to their sons. Identify them in Punnett squares and you'll score well.
intro: You are either XX or XY, and that one chromosomal difference shapes a huge amount of your biology. But sex chromosomes do more than determine biological sex — they also carry genes for other traits, and those genes are inherited in a very particular way. Colour blindness affects about 1 in 12 males but only 1 in 200 females. That dramatic difference isn't random — it's a predictable consequence of having just one X chromosome. This lesson explains sex determination and the inheritance pattern that makes X-linked conditions far more common in males.

keyPoints.heading: Sex chromosomes and sex determination
keyPoints.content: Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes in body cells. The 23rd pair are the sex chromosomes. Females have two X chromosomes (XX); males have one X and one Y chromosome (XY). The Y chromosome carries the SRY gene, which triggers male development. Since females are XX, all eggs carry an X chromosome. Males are XY, so sperm can carry either X or Y. At fertilisation, if an X sperm fertilises the egg, the offspring is XX (female); if a Y sperm fertilises the egg, the offspring is XY (male). A Punnett square shows this gives a 50% probability of each sex — though the actual ratio in any family is a chance outcome, not a guarantee.
writeBullets: Eggs always carry X (mother is XX)
Sperm carry either X or Y (father is XY)
X sperm + egg → XX (female); Y sperm + egg → XY (male)
Probability of each sex = 50% — a Punnett square shows this

keyTerms.def: The chromosomes that determine biological sex — XX in females, XY in males
The larger sex chromosome, carried by both males and females — females have two copies
The smaller sex chromosome, present only in males — carries genes for male sex determination

keyPoints.memTip: Female = XX (two kisses, like a letter sign-off). Male = XY (one X, then a Y — think of it as crossing into new territory). Eggs always give X; sperm are the variable — they decide the sex.

keyPoints.heading: Sex-linked genes — why males are more vulnerable
keyPoints.content: Some genes are located on the X chromosome but not on the Y chromosome. These are called sex-linked (or X-linked) genes. Because males have only one X chromosome, they have only one copy of these genes — there's no second X to provide a backup. This means that if a male inherits a recessive allele on his single X chromosome, there is no dominant allele to mask it, so the condition will be expressed. Females have two X chromosomes, so they need two copies of the recessive allele to show the condition. This is why X-linked recessive conditions (such as colour blindness and haemophilia) are much more common in males than in females.
writeBullets: Sex-linked gene = a gene located on the X chromosome but not on Y
Males (XY) have only one copy of X-linked genes — no backup allele
If a male inherits a recessive X-linked allele, it is always expressed
Females (XX) need two recessive copies to show the condition
X-linked recessive conditions are much more common in males than females

keyTerms.def: A gene located on the X chromosome that has no corresponding allele on the Y chromosome
A recessive allele on the X chromosome — affects males who inherit one copy, but only females who inherit two copies

keyPoints.memTip: Males have one X — one shot. If the allele on that X is recessive, there's nothing to override it. Think of the Y chromosome as an empty backup drive — it doesn't have the gene, so males can't compensate.

keyPoints.heading: Carrier females and Punnett squares for X-linked traits
keyPoints.content: A carrier female is heterozygous for an X-linked recessive allele (X^B X^b). She is phenotypically unaffected (the dominant allele masks the recessive one), but she can pass the recessive allele to her children. Use the notation: X^B for the dominant (normal) allele, X^b for the recessive (e.g. colour blindness) allele. For a cross between a carrier female (X^B X^b) and an unaffected male (X^B Y): daughters are X^B X^B (unaffected) or X^B X^b (carrier); sons are X^B Y (unaffected) or X^b Y (colour blind). So 50% of sons are affected, 50% of daughters are carriers. Males cannot be carriers for X-linked recessive conditions — they either show the condition or they don't.
writeBullets: Carrier female: X^B X^b — unaffected but carries recessive allele
Notation: X^B = dominant (normal); X^b = recessive (e.g. colour blind allele)
Carrier female × unaffected male: 50% of sons affected, 50% of daughters are carriers
Males cannot be carriers — with only one X, they either show the condition or don't
Affected male × unaffected female: all sons unaffected, all daughters are carriers

keyTerms.def: A female who is heterozygous for an X-linked recessive allele (X^B X^b) — unaffected but can pass the allele to children
An X-linked recessive condition affecting colour perception — far more common in males
An X-linked recessive condition in which blood does not clot properly — predominantly affects males

keyPoints.memTip: Carrier female: one good X, one dodgy X. She's fine, but watch out for her sons — each son has a 50:50 chance of getting the dodgy X and no backup. Her daughters can also pick up the carrier status silently.

tables.title: Carrier female × unaffected male — colour blindness cross

1.question: Why are males more commonly affected by X-linked recessive conditions than females?
options: Males produce more of the defective protein because they have more X chromosomes
Males have only one X chromosome — if it carries the recessive allele, there is no second X to mask it
The Y chromosome actively switches on recessive alleles
Females have a special immune response that prevents X-linked conditions
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — males have only one X chromosome. If the recessive allele is on it, there's no second X with a dominant allele to mask the condition. Females need two copies of the recessive allele to be affected.
1.wrongFeedback: The key is that males are XY — they only have one X chromosome. If that X carries the recessive allele, there's no backup dominant allele on the Y to override it. Females are XX, so they need both X chromosomes to carry the recessive allele before the condition shows.

2.question: A carrier female (X^B X^b) has children with an unaffected male (X^B Y). What is the probability that a son is colour blind?
options: 0% — sons inherit Y from their father, not the colour blind allele
25% — one in four of all children
100% of sons — they all get X^b from their mother
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — sons get their X from their mother and Y from their father. The mother is X^B X^b, so there's a 50% chance each son gets X^b — making him colour blind (X^b Y).
2.wrongFeedback: Sons get their X chromosome from their mother (not their father — the father only contributes Y to sons). The mother is X^B X^b, so each son has a 50% chance of inheriting X^b. If he does, he has no backup dominant allele and will be colour blind.

3.question: Can a male be a 'carrier' of an X-linked recessive condition without showing it?
options: Yes — males can carry one copy without being affected, just like females
No — males have only one X chromosome; if they carry the recessive allele, they show the condition
Yes — males carry the allele on their Y chromosome instead
No — males cannot inherit X-linked conditions at all
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — males have only one X chromosome, so there is no second copy to mask a recessive allele. If a male has the recessive allele on his X, he shows the condition. Carriers (unaffected but carrying the allele) can only be female.
3.wrongFeedback: Males cannot be carriers for X-linked recessive conditions. They have only one X chromosome, so any allele on it — dominant or recessive — is expressed. If a male has the recessive allele, he shows the condition. Only females (XX) can be carriers.

commonMistakes: Writing Y^b for a sex-linked allele — the recessive allele is on the X chromosome, not the Y. Males are X^b Y, not XY^b. The Y chromosome doesn't carry the gene at all.
Forgetting which parent sons and daughters inherit each sex chromosome from — sons get X from mum and Y from dad; daughters get one X from each parent.
Saying 50% of all children will be colour blind (instead of 50% of sons) — the condition is X-linked so you need to consider sex separately.
Describing carrier females as 'affected' — carrier females are phenotypically unaffected (X^B X^b). Only X^b X^b females would show the condition.
Confusing sex-linked with sex-limited — sex-linked means the gene is on a sex chromosome; sex-limited is a different concept where the trait only appears in one sex due to hormones.
examTips: Always show the full Punnett square with superscript notation — write X^B and X^b clearly. Examiners follow your working, so set it out methodically.
State your conclusions clearly: identify the sex of each genotype (e.g. 'X^b Y is a male with colour blindness') and whether females are carriers or affected.

When asked why males are more commonly affected, include: (1) males have only one X, (2) no second X to mask the recessive allele, (3) the recessive condition is therefore always expressed in males who carry it.

revisionCardBullets: Sex chromosomes: female = XX, male = XY
Eggs always carry X; sperm carry X or Y → determines sex of offspring
Probability of each sex = 50% (Punnett square with XX × XY)
Sex-linked gene = gene on X chromosome with no corresponding allele on Y
Males (XY) have one X — recessive X-linked allele always expressed (no backup)
Females (XX) need two recessive alleles to show X-linked condition
X-linked recessive conditions (colour blindness, haemophilia) far more common in males
Carrier female: X^B X^b — unaffected, but passes recessive allele to 50% of sons
Males cannot be carriers — they either show the condition (X^b Y) or don't (X^B Y)
In Punnett squares: sons inherit X from mum, Y from dad; daughters inherit one X from each parent
